text: This is a instructional video on a da Vinci XI right inguinal hernia repair.
I will be focused on performing a safe and thorough dissection.
The best way to ensure that your dissection is safe and thorough is to start with good port placement.
I like to place the ports in the upper abdomen, which means the camera is usually not in the location of the umbilicus.
The reason for this is because the umbilicus can vary in height relative to the pelvis.
In some patients, as in the illustration shown, the umbilicus is very low.
The surgeon makes a peritoneal incision at the level of the anterior superior iliac spine (ASIS), preparing the surgical field for further dissection.
In patients with a very low umbilicus, the camera positioned at the umbilicus level aligns directly with the peritoneal incision line.
Even if the umbilicus were slightly higher, you would have very little workspace to initiate your dissection, and also at the end of the case, you will have a difficult time with flap closure.
The surgeon identifies the anterior superior iliac spine (ASIS) as the landmark for camera placement, aligning it with the level of the peritoneal incision.
The camera is positioned twelve to fifteen centimeters above the peritoneal incision, providing an optimal view for the surgical procedure.
Therefore, the camera should be twelve to fifteen centimeters above the level of the ASIS.
This will give you a comfortable working distance from the peritoneal incision.
The camera port is positioned slightly off midline, entering through the rectus complex to utilize the rectus muscle and minimize the risk of a midline incisional hernia.
The surgeon positions the scissor and grasper ports at the same level as the camera, approximately eight centimeters away from the camera port.
You should use whatever method of entry is comfortable for you.
I prefer to use an optical entry to the right of midline in the case of a right angular hernia.
As a right-hander, I like to stand on the patient's left side.
The surgeon places the first robotic port in the right upper quadrant of the patient's abdomen, with the assistant positioned on the patient's right side.
The surgeon inserts a robot port, allowing the assistant to maneuver the camera to facilitate the placement of a port in the left upper quadrant and replace the initial camera port.
The surgeon infiltrates local anesthetic at the skin level and performs a rectus sheath block or mini tap block adjacent to the port site.
The surgeon inserts the robot port into the patient's abdomen until the dark band on the port is buried within the muscle tissue.
The camera is repositioned to the right upper quadrant port, providing a view from this location.
The surgeon places the left upper quadrant port, ensuring it is spaced approximately eight centimeters from the adjacent port.
The surgeon infiltrates the rectus and retro-rectus space with local anesthetic, visible as the needle is inserted adjacent to the port site.
The surgeon inserts the port until the dark band is buried within the muscle, positioning the remote center within the abdominal wall.
The initial optical trocar is upsized to an eight-millimeter robot port. The surgeon places the mesh and suture at the beginning of the procedure to enhance efficiency during the operation.
The surgeon performs a tap block or an angle of nerve block on the operating side while inserting the laparoscope.
The robot is docked at the pelvis, and a zero-degree scope is used to maintain a stable view without needing to adjust the orientation of the scope.
The surgeon inserts a fenestrated bipolar instrument in the left hand and a monopolar curved scissor in the right hand. The ports are burped to release tension before proceeding with the operation.
The surgeon begins the incision lateral to the linea semilunaris, firmly grasping the peritoneum to access the correct surgical plane.
The surgeon navigates through the surgical field, encountering a significant amount of preperitoneal fat near the abdominal wall, which decreases as they move medially.
The surgeon attempts to identify the areolar plane between the transverse salus fascia and the preperitoneal fascia, noting the thinness of the preperitoneal fascia against the peritoneum.
The surgeon attempts to dissect the preperitoneal fascia from the peritoneum, a challenging task due to the thinness of the fascia layer against the peritoneum.
The surgeon continues dissection through the areolar tissue, using instruments to spread the tissue easily within the correct anatomical plane.
The surgeon inspects the surgical field, identifying the space between the yellow preperitoneal fat and the orange fat, noting the minimal presence of vessels traversing this plane.
The surgeon identifies the orange fat as bladder tissue and the yellow fat as retro-rectus fat, ensuring the retro-rectus fat remains elevated during the procedure.
The surgeon moves laterally after establishing a small space within the areolar tissue, avoiding working in a confined area.
The surgeon carefully elevates the fibroareolar tissue laterally, staying close to the peritoneum and dissecting deep to the preperitoneal fascia.
The robotic system provides magnified visualization, revealing multiple unnamed fascial layers laterally during the dissection.
The surgeon identifies and incises the Shiozaki layer, the first of two layers, allowing for the displacement of surrounding fat tissue.
The surgeon identifies the Azure layer, which encompasses the testicular vessels and the peritoneum, revealing these structures within the surgical field.
The surgeon incises the Azure layer, separating the testicular vessels from the peritoneum to establish a safe plane between these structures.
The surgeon opens the Azure layer, allowing the testicular vessels to be gently pushed away, revealing the underlying anatomical structures.
The surgeon dissects the fibroareolar tissue, identified as the transverse salus fascia, from the peritoneum in a cephalad direction, without requiring precise movements.

The surgeon continues to divide the transverse salus fascia from the hernia sac. The fascia encircles the vas deferens and testicular vessels, requiring incision to facilitate the removal of the hernia sac.

The surgeon cauterizes safely between the ten and two o'clock positions, ensuring the vas deferens and testicular vessels remain posterior. Using the left hand, the surgeon pulls structures to the nine o'clock position while scraping with the right hand from three to four o'clock to unstack the anatomical layers.
The surgeon uses quick bursts of cautery to achieve hemostasis, controlling bleeding in the surgical field.
The surgeon attempts to follow the plane presented by the aerial tissue, but a noticeable amount of fat remains against the rectus muscle.
The surgeon positions anterior to the fat layer to access the rectus muscle, aiming to approach the pubis and Cooper's ligament while minimizing the risk of bladder injury.

The surgeon identifies the optimal plane for dissection, where tissue separates cleanly from the pubis and Cooper's ligament, facilitating the removal of structures without damaging surrounding areas.
The surgeon dissects past the midline, exposing the contralateral rectus muscle to ensure the mesh will adequately cover the direct space.

The surgeon maintains hemostasis during dissection to clearly identify critical structures like the bladder, ensuring safe tissue manipulation. The dissection extends two to three centimeters past the midline and two to three centimeters deep to Cooper's ligament.
The surgeon pulls tissue towards nine o'clock and strips towards three o'clock to reveal the peritoneal edge, ensuring visibility of the vas deferens to prevent accidental injury.
The surgeon approaches the most challenging area after dissecting the space of Retzius and the lateral space of Bogros, focusing on careful dissection to avoid injury to critical structures.
The surgeon carefully dissects near the bladder, iliac vein, and vas deferens, maintaining hemostasis and identifying natural areolar separations between the femoral fat and the fat on the bladder.
Again, hemostasis really is important to safe, thorough, and efficient dissection.
The surgeon checks internally to determine the endpoint of the sac, using a quick, short burst of cautery to maintain hemostasis during the procedure.
The surgeon identifies the vas deferens, using its location to guide the dissection plane. Hemostatic techniques reveal natural separation planes between the surrounding fat and critical structures.
The surgeon gently pulls and pushes through a substantial amount of fat, using cautery to identify and develop the areolar plane for safe dissection.
The surgeon carefully peels the peritoneum off the testicular vessels and the vas deferens, a process known as parietalizing the cord, revealing the underlying anatomical structures.
The surgeon carefully peels the peritoneum far back from the testicular vessels and vas, ensuring that the mesh will not sit on the peritoneum to prevent posterior recurrence.
The surgeon achieves hemostasis, revealing critical structures including the iliac vein, vas deferens, and testicular vessels within the surgical field.

The surgeon continues the posterior dissection, aiming for a four to five centimeter mesh overlap posteriorly and laterally. Good hemostasis reveals the peritoneal edge, which is carefully stripped off the testicular vessels and vas deferens.

The surgeon ensures that fibroareolar tissue is left over the lateral pelvic wall to protect the femoral branch, the genital branch of the genitofemoral nerve, and the lateral femoral cutaneous nerve. The width of the mesh is assessed to determine if the pocket size is adequate.

The completed dissection of the medial pubic obturator (MPO) allows the surgeon to position the mesh past the midline and towards the anterior superior iliac spine (ASIS). The mesh extends two centimeters beyond Cooper's ligament and approximately four centimeters posterior to the internal ring. Adequate space is visible in the space of Retzius for tucking the mesh between the bladder and pubis, while the transversalis fascia or fibroareolar tissue remains undisturbed laterally.

The surgeon inspects the surgical field for a core lipoma due to the absence of the ilipubic tract. The surgeon pulls on the fat but does not find a core lipoma. The medial umbilical ligament is identified as it connects to the iliac vein.

The surgeon ensures the mesh is securely tucked into the retropubic space to prevent the bladder from lifting it. The dissection extends well past the midline, allowing the mesh to lay flat against the left rectus muscle.
The surgeon replaces the scissors with a mega suture cut needle driver, preparing to perform suturing or needle manipulation in the surgical field.
The surgeon positions the pro grip mesh, ensuring it is appropriately aligned from left to right within the surgical field.
The surgeon unfolds the first flap of the mesh, ensuring it extends past the midline without pressing it firmly, allowing for potential repositioning.
The surgeon deploys the mesh by folding it, making it easier to handle. The mesh is then carefully unfolded at the crease, one flap at a time, ensuring proper placement.
The surgeon exposes the seam of the anatomical mesh, ensuring it is positioned at or just below the level of Cooper's ligament and approximately one to two centimeters posterior to the internal ring.
The surgeon unfolds the upper flap of the anatomical mesh, resulting in the complete opening of the lower flap.
The surgeon adjusts the mesh, ensuring it is positioned correctly between the superior pubic rami and the bladder, while confirming that the peritoneum is not trapped behind the mesh.
The surgeon completes unfolding the mesh and secures it in place, ensuring there is no peritoneum trapped behind the mesh. The peritoneum is adjusted to prevent proximity to the mesh.
The surgeon closes the peritoneal flap using a six-inch 3-0 V-Lock suture, starting from lateral to medial where the upper and lower edges of the flaps are closest together.

The surgeon carefully handles the thin lower flap of the mesh, aiming to efficiently close it within two minutes. The left hand pulls the needle out and presents it to the right hand to facilitate the closure process.
Don't drop the needle. If you have to let go of the needle, park it so that it's easy to re-grab it.
The surgeon's left hand presents the needle to the right hand, positioning it for efficient use in the working area.
The surgeon performs three to four suture throws before pulling the suture through, ensuring the closure is secure.
Whether you baseball stitch or canal stitch this, I don't think it really matters. You just want to be efficient and make sure there aren't gaps between your sutures.

I like to end at the medial and vocal ligament. This way the end of the suture will be protected by the medial and vocal ligament. I believe the end of the suture is usually where bowel obstructions occur. You want to lock the suture and then cut it flush against the peritoneum.

The surgeon repairs any visible peritoneal holes using a V-lock suture. For areas where the peritoneum appears thin, a figure-of-eight suture technique is employed to close the defect, as interrupted sutures are more prone to tearing.
The surgeon completes the repair by using figure-eight sutures with a V-lock, ensuring the closure of the peritoneal defect.
Thank you for watching.
